perturbation.original_text: 2.5 Sponsor's Fee For the services of the Sponsor as sponsor of the Offering and as full and complete compensation therefor, the Issuer will pay to the Sponsor the sum of $32,100 (inclusive of Goods and Services Tax), the receipt of which is acknowledged by the Sponsor.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 Sponsor's Fee For the services of the Sponsor as sponsor of the Offering and as full and complete compensation therefor, the Issuer will pay to the Sponsor a fee, the receipt of which is acknowledged by the Sponsor.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific amount of $32,100, the contract still mentions a 'fee,' but the amount is undefined. This creates uncertainty regarding the financial obligations of the Issuer and could lead to disputes. This contradicts section 7, which references 'costs and expenses in connection with the Offering' and indirectly relies on a defined Sponsor's Fee for overall cost calculations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. COSTS, EXPENSES AND TAXES Whether or not the Sponsor signs the certificate as contemplated in subsection 2.3, the Issuer will bear the costs and expenses in connection with the Offering, the preparation, execution and delivery of this Agreement, amendments to the Prospectus and the other documents to be delivered hereunder, including, without limitation: (a) the reasonable fees and out-of-pocket expenses of counsel for the Sponsor with respect thereto (which fees, not including expenses and taxes, are not expected to exceed $15,000 but may after consultation with and receipt of the prior approval of the Issuer) and with respect to advising the Sponsor as to its rights and responsibilities under this Agreement; (b) fees and costs of preparing and reproducing the Prospectus, any amendments thereto and any other Marketing Materials prepared by the Issuer; (c) filing fees in connection with compliance with the Securities Law; (d) all costs and expenses associated with obtaining an assessment report in compliance with Interim Local Policy Statement 3-17 of the British Columbia Securities Commission, if required; and (e) all costs and expenses, if any (including reasonable counsel fees and expenses), in connection with the enforcement of this Agreement, and the other documents to be delivered hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The removal of the specific fee amount in Section 2.5 creates a contradiction with Section 7, which refers to 'costs and expenses in connection with the Offering.' Because section 7 requires the overall cost calculation and now section 2.5 does not mention the fee amount, it creates uncertainty and contradicts section 7. Therefore, the change creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. INDEMNIFICATION 8.1 Indemnification of Indemnified Parties The Issuer shall and does hereby indemnify and save the Indemnified Parties harmless from and against any liability, claim, demand or loss, excluding loss of profits, which the Indemnified Parties may suffer, whether pursuant to statute or otherwise, howsoever arising, in consequence of: AG2432.386 [097] - 11 - (a) any statement or omission in the Prospectus, or otherwise made or omitted by the Issuer in connection with the Offering, being or being alleged to be a misrepresentation; (b) the Issuer not complying with any requirement of applicable legislation of Canada or of British Columbia or Alberta; or (c) any order made or any inquiry, investigation or proceeding commenced, threatened or announced by any securities regulatory authority or other competent authority in British Columbia, Alberta or Ontario which prevents or restricts trading in or the sale or distribution in British Columbia and Alberta of the Securities.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. INDEMNIFICATION 8.1 Indemnification of Indemnified Parties The Issuer shall indemnify and save the Indemnified Parties harmless from any liability, claim, demand or loss which the Indemnified Parties may suffer.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific clauses detailing the consequences leading to indemnification (misrepresentation in the Prospectus, non-compliance with legislation, regulatory actions), the general statement of indemnification lacks actionable specifics. This introduces a contradiction because while indemnification is mentioned, the conditions that trigger it are undefined. This contradicts the intent of clause 8.2 which discuss the right to counsel in case of claim contemplated by 'this section'. With no clear definition of when this section applies, clause 8.2 becomes ambiguous. It is also contradicting in 6.1, in the sense that Indemnification continues to be of full force, even if there is no full context on how the Indemnification will take place.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.2 Right to Counsel If any claim contemplated by this section is asserted against any of the Indemnified Parties, the Issuer shall be entitled (but not required) to assume the defence on behalf of the Indemnified Parties of any suit brought to enforce such claim, provided that the defence shall be through legal counsel acceptable to the Indemnified Parties and no admission of liability shall be made by the Issuer or the Indemnified Parties without, in each case, the prior written consent of all the parties hereto, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld. Any of the Indemnified Parties shall have the right to employ separate counsel in any such suit and participate in the defence thereof, at the expense of the Issuer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes specific conditions under which indemnification applies, significantly narrowing the scope established in the original text. This directly contradicts clause 8.2, which refers to 'any claim contemplated by this section'. Without defined conditions in section 8.1, the trigger for indemnification and the right to counsel in 8.2 become vague and unenforceable. The removal creates a direct and impactful contradiction that makes the application of 8.2 ambiguous.

perturbation.original_text: 5. EXPERT OPINIONS The Issuer shall deliver to the Sponsor on the date of filing the Prospectus: (a) a letter dated as of a date not more than one Business Day prior to the date of the Prospectus, in form and substance satisfactory to the Sponsor, from the then current auditor of the Issuer: (i) stating that, in such auditor's opinion, the financial statements and notes thereto of the Issuer examined by them and included in the Prospectus covered by his report therein comply as to form in all material respects with the applicable accounting requirements of the Securities Law; and (ii) stating that, in such auditor's opinion, the balance sheet of the Issuer examined by the auditor and included in the Prospectus and covered by his report therein complies as to form in all material respects with the applicable accounting requirements of the Securities Law; and (iii) addressing such other matters relating to the financial information in the Prospectus to which the Sponsor may reasonably require comfort; (b) a favourable legal opinion, in form and content reasonably satisfactory to the Sponsor, by counsel to the Issuer dated the date of the Prospectus and addressed to the Sponsor, relating to such legal matters as the Sponsor may reasonably request, including, without limitation, certain of the matters in section 3.1, title to the Issuer's property, and matters pertaining to the Securities Law; AG2432.386 [097] - 9 - (c) a certificate of the Issuer certifying certain facts relating to the business of the Issuer, and its affairs as may be reasonably requested by the Sponsor; and (d) any other certificates, comfort letters or opinions in connection with any matter related to the Prospectus which are reasonably requested by the Sponsor or their legal counsel.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. EXPERT OPINIONS The Issuer shall deliver to the Sponsor on the date of filing the Prospectus: (d) any other certificates, comfort letters or opinions in connection with any matter related to the Prospectus which are reasonably requested by the Sponsor or their legal counsel.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting clauses (a), (b) and (c) of Section 5, the contract removes the requirement for specific expert opinions (auditor's letter, legal opinion, and Issuer's certificate) while retaining the general clause (d) about 'any other certificates...reasonably requested'. This creates an in-text contradiction because clause (d) implicitly assumes that there is a base set of required certificates/opinions, which is no longer true. This contradicts section 2.2 Duty of Sponsor. In particular, it removes the duty for the Issuer to deliver crucial documents that are needed for an investigation to take place.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2 Duties of Sponsor As sponsor of the Issuer under the Prospectus, the Sponsor will conduct an investigation of the organization, management, business and affairs of the Issuer sufficient, in its sole discretion, to enable it to determine whether or not it is able to sign the certificate of the Prospectus.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the requirement for the Issuer to deliver specific expert opinions (auditor's letter, legal opinion, and Issuer's certificate) that were originally required in clauses (a), (b) and (c) of Section 5. This removal directly contradicts Section 2.2, which states that the Sponsor will conduct an investigation of the Issuer to enable it to sign the certificate of the Prospectus. Without the omitted expert opinions, the Sponsor cannot conduct a sufficient investigation, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
